segments.s365-g41: ز بس ... و از بس خروش is a causal/measure phrase: ز (poetic 'from/because of') + بس ('so much, to such an extent') quantifies the following nouns. بانگ اسپان ('the cry/shout of the horses') is a genitive construction where بانگ (sound/cry) is followed by the plural ezāfe-like modifier اسپان (horses). The second half repeats the pattern (از بس خروش) to mean 'and from so great a tumult'.
segments.s365-g42: همی here is the archaic narratorial/progressive particle ('kept' / continuous aspect) attached before the noun phrase ناله کوس, so the clause reads idiomatically as a single event: 'the ear did not hear the drum's lament.' ناله کوس is a noun+noun combination (lament of the drum). نشنید is past tense (negative) of شنیدن 'to hear', and گوش is the subject/affected noun 'ear' placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s365-g43: درفش فراوان برافراشته is a compact past participial/state description: درفش (banner) modified by فراوان (many/abundant) and followed by برافراشته, a past participle/adjective from برافراشتن 'to raise/erect' meaning 'having been raised' or 'raised high'. Word order places the object (banner) before its modifiers for poetic effect.
segments.s365-g44: همه نیزه‌ها ز ابر بگذاشته is another descriptive clause: همه نیزه‌ها ('all the spears') is the noun phrase, followed by ز ابر ('from/out of the cloud(s)') and بگذاشته, a past form of گذاشتن used here to indicate they were set/placed/appeared (poetically 'stood out'). The preposition ز gives a source/origin sense ('from/out of').
segments.s365-g45: چو introduces a simile ('like/as'). رسته is a past participle of رستن meaning 'sprung up/has grown', so چو رسته درخت از بر کوهسار reads 'like a tree that has grown/shot up on the mountainside.' از بر is a poetic locative: از (out of/from) + بر (on/upon) yields the sense 'on/upon the slopes of'.
segments.s365-g46: The second simile repeats چو ('like') and compares to a reed-thicket: بیشه نیستان is a compound 'grove/thicket of reeds'. به وقت بهار is a temporal phrase 'at the time of spring' with به وقت functioning as 'at the time of' and بهار 'spring (season)'.
segments.s365-g47: ازین سان ('in this way') is a fixed phrase (از + این + سان) meaning 'thus/so'. همی رفت is the classical past-progressive construction: همی gives continuous/ongoing aspect and رفت is the past of رفتن 'to go' — together meaning 'kept going' or 'went on'. گشتاسپ شاه is the name + title placed after the verb phrase for poetic inversion.
segments.s365-g48: ز کشور به کشور is a repeated-preposition expression meaning 'from land to land' (ز ... به ...). همی شد سپاه uses همی again for continuity and شد (past of شدن) meaning 'became/was formed' — here indicating that an army came into being or was mustered as he moved; سپاه ('army') is the result noun placed after the verb in inverted poetic order.
segments.s365-g9: سوی مرزدارانش نامه نوشت is a straightforward clause: نامه نوشت is a past-tense verbal phrase ('he wrote a letter'); سوی + مرزدارانش marks the indirect object/destination ('to his frontier-guards'). Word order is destination — object — verb, common in Persian narrative.
segments.s365-g10: که introduces reported content ('that'). خاقان is the subject 'the khaghan.' ره راد appears here as a proper-name/epithet (the text strings the two morphemes together as a name Rād or Rāh-Rād in poetic use), followed by مردی + بهشت in apposition: 'a man (worthy) of Paradise.' The copula is omitted, as typical in Persian narrative, so the noun phrases stand as a predicative description.
segments.s365-g11: بیایید is the 2nd-person plural imperative/ hortative 'come (you all)'. یکسر is an adverb modifying the imperative ('at once; all together'). به درگاه من is a locative phrase 'to my court/royal presence' with من as possessor.
segments.s365-g12: که introduces the reason clause ('for/because'). بر مرز بگذشت is a past-tense verbal phrase meaning 'passed over the border' (بگذشت = 'went past'). بد خواه من is a noun phrase 'an ill-wisher of mine' (بد modifies خواه, where خواه is used as 'wisher' — 'enemy/ill-wisher'); possession shown by من.
segments.s365-g13: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. نامه سوی راد مردان رسید: نامه (letter) + سوی (toward/to) + راد مردان ('rād men' — here 'rād' is adjectival/epithetic 'generous/brave' modifying مردان, 'men' or 'heroes') and رسید is the past-tense verb 'arrived/reached'. Word order is typical: time clause marker — object/recipient — verb.
segments.s365-g14: که again introduces the content/result clause 'that/so (then)'. آمد is simple past 'came'. جهانجوی is a compound adjective 'world-seeking' (ambitious, seeking dominion) modifying دشمن 'enemy'. پدید is a verbal/adjectival past participle meaning 'appeared/was made manifest' (here used as intransitive 'appeared').
segments.s365-g15: سپاهی functions as a collective subject ('an army' or 'troops'). بیامد is past tense 'came'. به درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the king's court' with درگاه شاه as a possessive/genitive construction.
segments.s365-g16: که introduces an explanatory/result clause: چندان 'so great' / 'to such an extent'; نبد is an archaic negative-past copula/verb meaning 'there was not' (negative of بود in classical/topical Persian). بر زمین بر گیاه repeats بر 'upon/on' to list locations 'on the earth, on the plants' — a poetic way of saying there had been no equal anywhere on earth or vegetation.
segments.s365-g17: ز بهر is an archaic poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'for' or 'for the sake of' (ز here = 'because/of/for' used with بهر = 'for'). جهانگیر is an epithet 'world‑conquering' modifying شاه (king); کیان names the royal house/dynasty (the Kayanids) and follows شاه as a post‑nominal identifier — together: 'the world‑conquering king of the Kayan (house)'.
segments.s365-g18: ببستند is a past plural verb 'they bound/assembled/arrayed' (poetic use of 'to bind' = 'to muster/assemble'); گردان is a collective noun 'troop/host'; گیتی میان is a locative phrase 'in the midst of the world' (گیتی = 'world', میان = 'middle/in the midst'), so the clause says they assembled troops in/throughout the world.
segments.s365-g19: به درگاه خسرو is a simple prepositional phrase 'to Khosrow's court' (به = 'to/for', درگاه = 'royal court', خسرو = 'Khosrow/the king'). نهادند روی is a common classical collocation: نهادند (they placed/set) + روی (face) = 'they set their face toward' i.e. 'they turned their faces to/they directed themselves to'. The verb is past plural.
segments.s365-g20: همه مرزداران = 'all the border-wardens/frontier-guards' (همه quantifies the noun). به فرمان اوی is an instrumental/agentive phrase 'by his command' (به used here with instrumental meaning, فرمان = 'command', اوی = archaic clitic for 'his'). The order is topical: 'all the frontier-guards, by his command.'
segments.s365-g21: برین is an archaic/deictic combination (بر + این) meaning roughly 'to/at this (place or time)'; برنیامد is a past negative form literally 'did not come/appear' but in context functions idiomatically with بسی روزگار to speak of the passage of many days ('many days went by'). بسی روزگار = 'many a time/much time' (time expression).
segments.s365-g22: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause 'that/when'; گرد here is used poetically as a collective 'host/army' (though the listed senses are more basic), followed by از گزیده هزاران هزار 'of chosen thousands and thousands' — a genitive/partitive phrase specifying the host as composed of chosen men. The line therefore describes hosts made up of selected thousands.
segments.s365-g23: فراز آمده بود is a past (pluperfect/compound) expression 'had come up / had appeared on high' (فراز = 'on high/above', آمده بود = 'had come/was come'). مر here is an archaic pronominal element related to 'me' that in older usage can function as a possessive/associative marker before شاه (so the phrase centers on 'the (my/that) king'); را marks شاه as definite/emphatic. Word order is elevated/poetic: adverbial (فراز) + perfective compound + object/person reference.
segments.s365-g24: کی is a relative/interrogative 'who' (here introducing a descriptive phrase); نامدار و نکو are coordinated adjectives 'renowned and good/excellent'; خواه is the substantive/participle 'seeker/desirer' (i.e., 'one who seeks'), so نامدار و نکو‌خواه = 'one renowned and desiring of good.' The final را again marks or emphasizes the referent. Together: 'one who was famed and sought goodness.'
segments.s365-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی آمد is a verbal clause 'news/information came'; به marks the recipient ('to'), so the whole phrase reads literally 'when information came to Gushtasp, the king' with شاه appended as a title after the proper name.
segments.s365-g2: که introduces the content of the news ('that...'). سالار چین is a noun phrase 'the commander of China' (genitive: China after the title). جملگی با سپاه is an adverbial phrase 'altogether/entirely with the army' qualifying the subject; word order places the descriptive adverbial after the noun phrase.
segments.s365-g3: بی‌آراسته آمد is a past-tense clause 'came unadorned / came without preparation' (بی‌آراسته = not adorned/unprepared). از جای خویش is a source phrase 'from his place'; خویش is expressed here as خوی + ش (possessive clitic), so جای خویش = 'his place.'
segments.s365-g4: This is a transitive clause: خشاش is here a proper name (not the botanical sense) and thus no supplied common-noun sense fits; یلش means 'his hero/warrior' (یل + possessive), را is the definite-object marker, فرستاد is the past tense 'sent/dispatch(ed)' and پیش indicates 'forward / ahead / before' — so 'Khashash sent his hero forward.'
segments.s365-g5: چو بشنید is a temporal clause 'when he heard' (چو = when, بشنید = he heard). The following کو here functions as an archaic/poetic subordinating particle meaning 'that / who' introducing the reported clause 'had gone with his army' (رفت با لشکرش).
segments.s365-g6: که here introduces the purpose/result clause 'in order to / that (he) would destroy'. ویران + کند uses ویران as the lexical root 'ruin' and کند (present/subjunctive/optative form of کردن) expresses intention or purpose ('may/should/would destroy'); آن نکو کشورش is a noun phrase 'that fair (noble) country of his' with آن determiner and possessive -ش.
segments.s365-g7: سپهبدش را گفت is 'he said/told to his spahbed' — را marks the addressee as definite recipient of the speech. The time adverbial فردا پگاه ('tomorrow at dawn') follows to mark when the commanded action should occur; Persian often places temporal phrases after the reported verb.
segments.s365-g8: This is a pair of imperatives giving orders. بیارای here functions as an imperative 'array/prepare' (archaic/poetic form) taking پیل 'elephant(s)' as its object; و بیاور is the second imperative 'and bring' with سپاه 'the army' as its object. The conjunction و links the two commands.
segments.s365-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (classical/poetic). روزی functions as a time adverb 'one day' (literally ‘a day dawned’ in older usage). ببخشید is 3rd-person past (he bestowed/gave) and is coordinated with the next verb: the clause means 'when, one day, he gave/ bestow(ed) and he gave a cuirass.' جوشن is the direct object 'cuirass/armor' and بداد here continues the past action (he gave); the two past verbs are paratactic, sharing the same subject.
segments.s365-g34: This is a sequence of two past actions. بزد is 3rd-person past 'he struck' (literary past). The coordinated objects نای و کوس are musical/war instruments (ney flute and war-drum) listed with و. بنه is another noun put in series with the instruments (meaning uncertain in this list), then the phrase بر نهاد contains the preposition بر 'on/upon' plus نهاد, 3rd-person past of نهادن 'to place/put', so 'he struck the ney and the drum and placed the … on (something)'.
segments.s365-g35: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past meaning 'he ordered/commanded' (literary). It is followed by the infinitive بردن 'to carry/to take', the complement of the command. ز پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from/before the front of the army' (ز پیش = before/in front of), specifying where the carrying should occur.
segments.s365-g36: A nominal phrase in apposition: درفش 'banner/standard' is the head noun, modified by the epithets همایون فرخنده 'auspicious/fortunate' that follow it, and finally شاه 'of the king' marks possession (the banner belonging to the king). In Persian poetry epithets commonly cluster around the noun in this way.
segments.s365-g37: سوی is the preposition 'toward'; رزم ارجاسپ is 'the battle of Arjasp' (noun + proper name). لشکر کشید is a compact verbal expression: لشکر is the military force and کشید is 3rd-person past of کشیدن; here کشید has the idiomatic sense 'moved/mustered/marched' (i.e. 'marshaled an army'). Word order is compressed as typical in poetry.
segments.s365-g38: سپاهی is the noun 'a host/force' (here 'a force' or 'troops'). که introduces a relative clause 'that ...' and هرگز ('never') negates the past verb ندید 'saw'. چنان کس ندید = 'that no one ever saw such (a thing)' — the relative clause modifies سپاهی. Note the classical negation pattern: هرگز + کسی/کس + negated past verb.
segments.s365-g39: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' indicating cause. تاریکی 'darkness' is followed by و and گرد پای سپاه 'the dust at the army's feet' (گرد = dust, پای سپاه = the army's foot/at the army's feet). This whole phrase gives the cause/setting for the following negative statement.
segments.s365-g40: کسی with a following negative verb ندید creates the sense 'no one saw' (here کسی = 'no one'). روز روشن is 'clear/bright day' (object), and ایچ is an emphatic/negative particle 'at all' (older/poetic). راه 'road' frames the location; together the clause means 'no one at all saw daylight on the road.' Note the classical order where the negative sense is carried by the verb together with the existential pronoun.
segments.s365-g25: به لشکرگه آمد is a prepositional phrase ('به' = toward/into) + intransitive past verb 'آمد' (he came). سپه را بدید is a separate past clause: 'سپه' (the host) is marked by the object marker 'را' and the verb 'بدید' is an archaic past form of دیدن (he saw). The subject is omitted and carried over from the narrative (typical in Persian epic style).
segments.s365-g26: که introduces a subordinate clause describing what he did next. The noun phrase شایسته بد رزم را is the object: شایسته ('worthy/appropriate') modifies the following adjective+noun بد رزم ('bad/ill at battle'); 'را' marks the whole phrase as definite object. برگزید is the simple past 'he chose/selected'. The clause’s subject is again omitted and understood from the prior clause.
segments.s365-g27: ازان is a connective adverb ('from that/thereupon') pointing back to the previous action. شادمان گشت is a copular/became-construction: شادمان ('happy') + گشت (became). فرخنده شاه is an epithet + noun (fortunate/auspicious king) in apposition.
segments.s365-g28: دلش خیره آمد is an idiomatic sequence: دلش ('his heart' = دل + enclitic possessive ش) + خیره آمد ('came fixed/was astonished' — خیره here = astonished/stunned and آمد = came/occurred, forming ‘became astonished’). زبی مر سپاه is a short poetic prepositional phrase: زبی ('from/at' in poetic usage) + مر (a clitic pronoun here meaning 'to me' or 'before me') + سپاه ('the army'), i.e. 'astonished at the army before him.'
segments.s365-g29: دگر روز is an adverbial time expression ('another/next day'); گشتاسپ is the subject (proper name). با موبدان is a prepositional phrase ('with the mobeds/priests') listing companions. Word order is regular: time — subject — accompaniment.
segments.s365-g30: This segment is an enumerative coordination: ردان و بزرگان و اسپهبدان are nouns joined by و ('and'), continuing the list started by 'با موبدان' (i.e. 'with the ...'). Each noun is plural/collective and functions as part of the compound accompaniment of the subject.
segments.s365-g31: This is a compact clause in which actions and agent are arranged in classical order: گشاد (an adjective/participial 'opened' / 'gaping/open') + آن در ('that door' — در here = 'door/gate') + گنج پر کرده ('having filled the treasure' — گنج 'treasury', پر 'full (of)', کرده is a perfective/participial form 'having made/filled'). جم (Jam) appears at the end as the agent performing these actions, a common postposed subject in epic style.
segments.s365-g32: سپه را بداد is the core transfer clause: سپه را ('the army', with object marker را) + بداد — here an archaic past form of دادن ('he gave') used in epic Persian (note: this exact verbal sense is not present in the supplied sense list for this token). او is the explicit subject pronoun ('he') placed after the verb (permitted in poetic syntax). دو ساله درم is a measure phrase meaning 'two years' worth of dirhams' (ساله = yearly/annual, درم = dirham coin), indicating the amount or pay given.

sentences.s365-s21: Because of the horses' shouting and the great tumult, the ear did not hear the drum's lament.
sentences.s365-s22: Many banners were raised, and all the spears stood out from the clouds.
sentences.s365-s23: Like a tree that has sprung up on the mountainside, like a reed-grove at the time of spring.
sentences.s365-s24: Thus King Gushtasp went on in this way, moving from realm to realm, and an army was continually assembled.
sentences.s365-s5: He wrote a letter to his frontier-guards, saying that the khaghan Rād was a man of Paradise.
sentences.s365-s6: Come at once to my court, for whoever crosses the border is an ill-wisher of mine.
sentences.s365-s7: When the letter reached the rād men, the world-seeking enemy appeared.
sentences.s365-s8: An army came to the king's court; such a multitude had not existed on the earth or on the plants.
sentences.s365-s9: For the world-conquering king of the Kayanids they marshaled troops across the world.
sentences.s365-s10: They turned their faces to Khosrow's court — all the frontier-wardens, at his command.
sentences.s365-s11: Many days passed, during which hosts of the chosen — thousands upon thousands — were gathered.
sentences.s365-s12: Before the king there had come up on high one who was renowned and who sought the good.
sentences.s365-s1: When news came to King Gushtasp that the commander of China, all together with his army, ...
sentences.s365-s2: He came forth unadorned from his place and Khashash sent his hero forward.
sentences.s365-s3: When he heard that he had gone with his army, (that) to destroy that fair country of his, ...
sentences.s365-s4: He told his spahbed, 'Tomorrow at dawn, array the elephants and bring the army.'
sentences.s365-s17: When one day he bestowed gifts and gave a cuirass, he struck the ney and the drum and set a standard on high.
sentences.s365-s18: He commanded that the felicitous royal standard be carried before the army.
sentences.s365-s19: Toward battle Arjasp marshaled his army — a force such as no one had ever seen.
sentences.s365-s20: Because of the darkness and the dust at the army's feet, no one at all saw daylight on the road.
sentences.s365-s13: He came to the army-camp, saw the host, and selected those ill-suited for combat.
sentences.s365-s14: Thereupon the fortunate king became joyful; his heart was astonished at the army before him.
sentences.s365-s15: The next day Gushtasp, with the mobeds, the radan, the great men, and the spahbeds...
sentences.s365-s16: Jam opened that door of the treasure-house, filled it, and gave the army two years' worth of dirhams.
